era_context: Middle Bronze Age. The Hittite Old Kingdom is consolidating power in Anatolia. The Hyksos Fifteenth Dynasty controls the Nile Delta and Lower Egypt. The Kassite dynasty rules Babylon and southern Mesopotamia. Mycenaean Greece is in the Early Bronze Age III. The Indus Valley Civilization is in post-urban decline. This is a period of regional powers and political fragmentation, with no single dominant empire.

events.title: Hittite Old Kingdom: continued consolidation and regional expansion
events.description: The Hittite Old Kingdom continues its consolidation of power in central Anatolia and expansion into neighboring regions. The Hittites are establishing administrative structures, military organization, and cuneiform literacy. This period sees the emergence of Hittite as a distinct political and cultural entity, though the empire has not yet achieved the territorial extent and military dominance of the New Kingdom period.

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and later compilations (Telepinu Edict)

events.certainty_note: The Hittite Old Kingdom's general trajectory is well-attested, but specific events for the year -1597 are not documented.
cross_references: Part of Hittite Old Kingdom period (c. 1650–1620 BCE)

events.title: Kassite Babylon: administrative and cultural integration
events.description: The Kassite dynasty continues to integrate Kassite military and administrative elites with Babylonian cultural and religious traditions. The Kassites adopt cuneiform writing, Babylonian law codes, and religious practices, while maintaining their own language and ethnic identity. This cultural synthesis will characterize the Kassite period for the next 400+ years.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Babylon and Mesopotamian sites
Babylonian king lists and administrative texts (later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Kassite cultural integration is well-attested, but specific events for the year -1597 are not documented.
cross_references: Part of early Kassite period (c. 1595–1400 BCE)

events.title: Hyksos Fifteenth Dynasty: continued rule in Egypt
events.description: The Hyksos Fifteenth Dynasty continues its rule over the Nile Delta and Lower Egypt. The Hyksos maintain administrative centers, military garrisons, and trade networks. The native Egyptian Thirteenth Dynasty continues to rule Upper Egypt with diminished authority. This period of divided rule will continue until the New Kingdom Egyptian expansion under Ahmose I (c. 1550 BCE).
key_figures: Hyksos kings of the Fifteenth Dynasty

sources.name: Egyptian New Kingdom texts describing the Hyksos period (later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The Hyksos period is well-attested archaeologically, but specific events for the year -1597 are not documented.
cross_references: Part of Hyksos rule in Egypt (c. 1650–1550 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: The year -1597 falls within a period of very sparse contemporary documentation. Specific events for this year are essentially unknown. The entries above describe broader patterns of political consolidation and cultural integration occurring during the early Kassite, Hittite Old Kingdom, and Hyksos periods. All dating for this period is subject to significant chronological uncertainty (±20–50 years).

graph_edges.note: Hittite and Kassite consolidation occur simultaneously
Hyksos rule in Egypt is contemporary with Hittite and Kassite consolidation
